News.title: Person killed & another injured after train crashes into car at level crossing
News.content:
TRAIN SMASH Person killed & another injured after train crashes into car at level crossing

ONE person has died and another left injured after a train crashed into a car at a level crossing.

Emergency services raced to the scene in Ely, Cambridgeshire on Tuesday morning.



British Transport Police said they were called to reports of a collision between a train and a car on the Dimmocks Cote level crossing in Stretham at around 10am.

Despite the best efforts of paramedics, one person was tragically pronounced dead at the scene.



Another was treated for minor injuries with police now investigating the incident, urging any witnesses to come forward.

As a result of the crash, rail passengers have been warned of travel disruption through Cambridgeshire which is expected to last throughout the afternoon.

Lines between Waterbeach and Ely were suspended as were trains between Cambridge North and Ely.

Rail operator CrossCountry, said they were working alongside emergency services and Network Rail.



A spokesperson for BTP said: “BTP were called at around 10.15am this morning to reports of a collision involving a train and a car at Dimmocks Cote level crossing in Ely.

“Officers responded alongside paramedics and sadly a person has been pronounced dead at the scene.”

“Enquiries are ongoing to identify the person and inform their next of kin. Another person is receiving treatment for minor injuries.

“Enquiries are ongoing to establish the full circumstances of what happened, any witnesses who haven’t already spoken to police are asked to text BTP on 61016 quoting the reference 198 of March 3.”

News.title: Moment fighting couple having furious lovers’ spat are hauled off plane before takeoff after argument caused 40min delay
News.content:
THIS is the moment an arguing couple were kicked off a plane after their lovers’ spat caused a 40 minute delay.

The pair had boarded the flight before becoming embroiled in a heated disagreement shortly before take off.





Other passengers caught the quarrel on video as the Thai VietJet Air plane remained grounded at Taiwan Taoyuan International Airport in Taipei.

The husband was seen shouting at his wife, causing flight attendants to issue a formal warning.

Outside intervention made the conflict escalate with the couple turning their ire towards the cabin crew.



In the video, the man can be seen recording three crew members during the altercation.

According to fellow traveller Wilaiwan Chantabng, the couple had originally agreed to travel together; however, as the plane prepared to take off, the husband suddenly changed his mind.

“They argued with the staff and said that they had paid for their tickets just like everyone else,” he said.

“He then walked around the aircraft filming everything two or three times.”



The couple was eventually escorted off the plane by security.

Scheduled to fly from the Taiwanese capital to Suvarnabhumi International Airport in Bangkok, Thailand, the journey normally takes around three hours and 55 minutes.

Due to the couple’s argument, the flight only reached its final destination after five hours.

The delay comes after a Ryanair flight was forced to U-Turn mid-air after a passenger had a drunken meltdown.

The flight from Madrid to Ibiza was delayed by two hours after the intoxicated traveller refused to turn off his mobile phone.



Shocking video emerged, showing staff trying to calm the man down, but he refused to stop talking on the phone.

Footage shows the man shouting as cabin crew try to de-escalate the situation.

The man continued to refuse to listen to instructions to turn his phone off.

The drunk passenger was heard hitting the cabin and causing chaos onboard.

Another passenger told the Majorca Daily Bulletin about the dramatic scene.



They said the troublemaker was already talking loudly on the phone and shouting when they boarded the 22.15 flight.

The traveller who reported the incident said: “We were boarding and already saw the young man making a scene, talking loudly on the phone, shouting, and everyone was watching him.”

“He kept using his phone, and then they reprimanded him,” the witness said.

“The plane had to stop, attendants approached him, he took off his seatbelt, until a fellow passenger told him enough was enough.”

They called the scene “chaotic” as the exchange between staff and the passenger escalated.



RECOMMENDED STORIES

Officers from the local Guardia Civil eventually removed the man from the plane.

News.title: First UK evacuation flight from Middle East to take off ‘in days’ as ‘unprecedented’ 130k Brits sign up amid Iran crisis
News.content:
BRITAIN’S first rescue flight will depart Oman within days as the UK government scrambles to save 130,000 Brits stranded in a brutal Middle Eastern conflict.

Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper announced to the Commons that a government-chartered plane will depart Muscat “in the coming days”.





Cooper also revealed that an “unprecedented” 130,000 British nationals have registered their presence in the region this morning.

Around one million people have been affected by a blanket closure of the Gulf’s airspace after the coordinated US-Israeli blitzing of Iran on Saturday.

Frightened Brits have been forced to hunker down and take cover as brutal Iranian bombing campaigns plague cities across the Middle East.



It comes as…

Read more about Iran SOS FLIGHT First UK evacuation flight from Middle East to take off 'in days' amid crisis

Well-off holidaymakers are forking out as much as £100,000 to flee the United Arab Emirates as more than 11,000 flights have been cancelled since the beginning of the conflict.

Britain is still cooking up an evacuation mission, but appears to be hoping that commercial airspace will reopen.

Addressing the House of commons this morning, Cooper said: “We are also working with airlines on increasing capacity out of Muscat for British nationals, with priority for vulnerable nationals”.



She added that said individuals will be contacted by the foreign office regarding these options, and that work will continue around the clock to support stranded Brits.

Cooper also told MPs that is it a “very fast-moving situation” with “unprecedented numbers of British nationals in the region”.

“As of this morning, 130,000 British nationals have now signed up to the Register Your Presence Programme, which is vital to the Foreign Office’s ability to know where people are and to provide updates and advice,” she said.

“I am in close contact with my counterparts across the region, speaking again yesterday with the UAE on the excellent support that they are providing and some of the departures that they are now securing as this becomes viable”.

Gulf states including the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Bahrain and Kuwait have been caught in the crossfire as Tehran targets US bases across the region.



One person was killed and 11 others injured at airports in Dubai and Abu Dhabi after they were struck amid last weekend’s mayhem.

The former’s plush sever-star Burj al Arab became in engulfed in flames after it was attacked by a suspected kamikaze drone.

Dubai International – one of the busiest transport hubs in the world – and the other main airport in the city announced they were resuming a limited number of flights on Monday evening.

Despite this, some desperate tourists have decided to try and get out on their own terms.



Some have forked out tens of thousands for private jets to take them out of the country, while others have driven more than 10 hours to reach open airports in Oman and Saudi Arabia, Forbes reports.

The demand for chartering private flights has shot up over the last few days as few operators remain willing to keep operations ongoing in the region, the outlet reported.

Many local aircraft usually used for private flights are reportedly now stuck in closed airports in the region.

This has forced private operators to be bring in jets from further away – significantly ramping up costs.



Airports in Muscat, Oman, and Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, have become key travel hubs for those looking to get out quickly.

Most flights going out of the region are heading to Istanbul, London and Rome, the outlet said.

Jet flights from Muscat to Istanbul are reportedly up for grabs at eye-watering fees of more than £70,000 – around double the normal rate.



Meanwhile, heavy jet charters are reaching up to £105,000.

Private security firms have also been hiring SUVs to deliver clients to open airports to board private flights.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

It comes after Cristiano Ronaldo’s luxury £61million private jet left Saudi Arabia in the middle of the night as bombs rained down on the US embassy in Riyadh.

Ronaldo, 41, lives in the Saudi capital with his wife, Georgina Rodriguez, and his five children, but it is so far unclear whether they were aboard the plane.

News.title: Prince Harry offers Beatrice and Eugenie sanctuary at his LA home amid Andrew arrest
News.content:
Prince Harry offers Beatrice and Eugenie sanctuary at his LA home amid Andrew arrest

The Duke of Sussex have offered a safe space in America for his cousins, as they continue to grapple with the fallout from their father’s arrest

View 4 Images Prince Harry has maintained a good relationship with Beatrice and Eugenie since leaving the Firm (Image: UK Press via Getty Images)

Prince Harry has extended a generous offer to his cousins, Princesses Beatrice and Eugenie, by giving them a safe space to escape the fallout from their father’s arrest in the UK. The Duke of Sussex has offered the princesses sanctuary at his home in Montecito, which he shares with his wife Meghan Markle and their two children, Archie and Lilibet.

All eyes have been on Beatrice and Eugenie as the scandal surrounding their parents continues, with Sarah Ferguson reportedly laying low at a wellness retreat in Ireland, while Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor is believed to be laying low at his home at Sandringham after he was arrested on suspicion of misconduct in public office.

With the spotlight now on the sisters to react to the Epstein scandal that has engulfed their parents, their cousin Harry has extended a generous offer to keep them away from prying eyes in their home country.

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

View 4 Images Princess Beatrice and Princess Eugenie have kept a low profile since their father's arrest (Image: Getty Images)

A source told the Mail on Sunday : "Harry has communicated with the girls, saying something along the lines of, 'He knows what it's like to be at the wrong end of the institution'. He's said there's an open invite, especially for Beatrice, if she ever wants one."

Harry has remained close to his cousin Beatrice since he and Meghan quit their royal duties and relocated to America in 2020. The 37-year-old, who has two young daughters, is understood to have taken her parents' fall from grace particularly hard.

The Duke of Sussex and Princess Eugenie have also maintained a close relationship, as they always had throughout their early adult years, with Eugenie and Meghan reportedly having a very friendly bond.

View 4 Images Prince Harry has offered his cousins sanctuary at his home in LA (Image: Wiktor Szymanowicz/Future Publishing via Getty Images)

The scandal surrounding Mountbatten-Windsor has continued, with calls for the former prince to be formally removed from the line of succession. The UK government has confirmed they are discussing the move, which would take place after any active investigations into the former prince have concluded.

As a result, some royal watchers have started to question if Beatrice and Eugenie, as well as their children, would also be removed from the line of succession. Beatrice is currently ninth in line for the throne, while Eugenie is in 12th place behind Beatrice’s children.

The princesses have only broken their cover recently, being seen out in public since the latest tranche of the Epstein files were released. While there is no suggestion the princesses were involved in any wrongdoing, they have been embroiled in the scandal by association to Andrew and Fergie.

View 4 Images Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor was arrested last month (Image: PA)

In the wake of the scandal, Beatrice and Eugenie have reportedly been told that they cannot attend Royal Ascot in June this year, with the princesses being “completely blindsided” by the decision.

Article continues below

A source, according to the Daily Mail, claimed: "I've spoken to my friend who works at Ascot and they said the girls have been told they can't be there this year. Beatrice has taken it the hardest. She's been completely blindsided by all of this."

Another source allegedly said: "Ascot would be out of the question because the royals have been told they can't have pictures with the girls for the rest of the year."

News.title: Refund rights for cancelled Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Qatar flights
News.content:
Refund rights for cancelled Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Qatar flights

People have been given advice on what to do about claiming compensation

View 2 Images Holidaymakers have been told to shelter in place (Image: © Christopher Pike/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

People should know their rights about what they can legally claim as a result of travel plans being disrupted, delayed or cancelled altogether. Reminders have been issued due to the ongoing conflict in parts of the Middle East.

‌



Major airports and airspaces have been closed or restricted in recent days. Flights from parts of the UAE, including Dubai and Abu Dhabi, along with Qatar, Kuwait and Bahrain have been suspended or cancelled. Holidaymakers visiting these destinations may have had their travel plans to get home put on hold due to the military action in the region.

‌



During the changes as people prepare to be rerouted, they should know exactly what claims to make to reclaim some of their money in compensation. Which?, the consumer rights champion, shared advice to anyone affected on its website.

‌



"The situation is developing very quickly. If you’re due to travel to or via affected areas in the coming days, you should monitor your airline’s website to determine whether your travel plans will be affected — and your options, if so," it said.

"Keep an eye on the Foreign Commonwealth Development Office (FCDO) page for your destination and any countries you’re due to transit through. You should also make sure you have travel insurance from a reputable provider."

Content cannot be displayed without consent

‌



Rights to help and refunds over flight changes

For people already in the affected areas, the priority at the moment should be following the "shelter in place" alert across the UAE, Qatar, Bahrain or Kuwait. A Which? spokesperson explained: "Passengers flying on UK or European airlines, or on any airline departing the UK or EU, are legally entitled to choose between rebooking or a refund. They also have a duty of care to passengers, providing or reimbursing passengers for food, hotel accommodation and transport costs."

If you are travelling with an airline that is not British or European, such as Etihad or Emirates, and are not flying into or out of Europe, you may encounter more difficulties, as the EU and UK’s air passenger rights do not extend to your situation. Contact your airline or travel agent to discuss your options.

The Which? team added: "If you are [currently] trying to get home, don't accept a refund. This ends the airlines duty of care and you will be liable for food and hotel costs."

‌



View 2 Images People have been urged to know their rights and not to accept refunds until they get home (Image: NurPhoto via Getty Images)

Should I cancel my upcoming trip?

Which? advised against making any cancellations - even if it looks unlikely that you'll be able to travel. A spokesperson said: "This is because your rights to a full refund, rebooking and assistance are usually surrendered if you cancel the flight. It may also complicate matters if you attempt to make a claim on your insurance at a later date."

Before making any changes to your booking, get in touch with your airline or travel company and ask them about your options.

Article continues below

People who have booked travel and accommodation separately should see if hotels are offering free cancellations. Which? reports that some travellers were able to get their stay refunded or "moved at the host’s discretion".

News.title: Everyone taking statins needs to know about ezetimibe
News.content:
Everyone taking statins needs to know about ezetimibe

The rule could save the lives of thousands of people every year

View 4 Images Millions of statins are taken every year in the UK

Thousands of lives could be saved by changing the rules around prescribing statins, according to research. The largest analysis to examine the best way to lower levels of "bad" cholesterol in patients with blocked arteries shows that they should immediately be given a combination of a statin and another drug, called ezetimibe, rather than statins alone.

‌



The combination therapy should be considered the "gold standard" of treatment for patients who have already had a heart attack and could prevent thousands of deaths every year, say scientists. Researchers analysed the findings of 14 previous studies involving 108,353 patients who were at "very high" risk of a heart attack or stroke, or who had already suffered one of the other.

‌



The study, published in the journal Mayo Clinic Proceedings, showsed that when ezetimibe was combined with a high dose statin to reduce levels of low density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C), there was a "significant" 19% reduction in the risk of death from any cause.

‌



View 4 Images Rosuvastatin is one of the most common statins prescribed in the UK

There was also a 16% reduction in deaths from cardiovascular causes, and a "significant" reduction in the major adverse cardiovascular events or stroke by 18% and 17% respectively, compared to high doses of statins alone. The combination therapy also significantly reduced LDL-C levels by an extra 13mg per decilitre (dL) of blood compared to statins alone, measured from the baseline – the time at which the patient first started the treatment.

That increased the chances of reaching the ideal goal of less than 70mg/dL of LDL-C by 85%, according to the findings. Study first author Professor Maciej Banach, of the John Paul II Catholic University of Lublin, Poland, said: “These results were even more pronounced in the network meta-analysis, which enables a direct comparison of different therapy regimens used in the study.

‌



"This showed a 49% reduction in all-cause mortality and a 39% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events, when compared to high dose statin therapy alone.” Prof Banach, who also leads the International Lipid Expert Panel and the Blood Pressure Meta-analysis Collaboration group that conducted the study, said: “The combination therapy is safe and efficacious.

"The risk of adverse events and the therapy discontinuation rate was comparable between groups. "In the network meta-analysis, we showed a significant 44% reduction in the risk of discontinuation in those treated with moderately high dose statin therapy plus ezetimibe versus a high dose statin alone.”

View 4 Images A prescription bottle of Lipitor (atorvastain calcium) tablets

‌



Until now, there had been inconsistent findings about whether or not combined cholesterol lowering therapy should be given to high risk patients immediately, even before they suffer a heart attack or stroke. Study co-author Professor Peter Toth, of the University of Illinois in the US, said: “This study confirms that combined cholesterol lowering therapy should be considered immediately and should be the gold standard for treatment of very high-risk patients after an acute cardiovascular event.

"Simply adding ezetimibe to statin therapy, without waiting for at least two months to see the effects of statin monotherapy, which is suboptimal in many patients, is associated with more effective LDL-C goal achievement and is responsible for significant incremental reductions in cardiovascular health problems and deaths.

‌



“This approach does not require additional funding or reimbursement of new expensive drugs. "In fact, it may translate into lower rates of first and subsequent heart attacks and stroke, and their complications like heart failure, which are extremely costly for all healthcare systems.”

Figures show that around 4.5million deaths a year are a result of high LDL-C. Prof. Banach said: “Cardiovascular disease kills around 20 million people a year worldwide.

View 4 Images Statins are a common medication in the UK

‌



"Based on our previous analysis, we estimate that if combination therapy to reduce LDL-C was included in all treatment guidelines and implemented by doctors everywhere for patients with high cholesterol levels, it would prevent over 330,000 deaths a year among patients who have already suffered a heart attack, and almost 50,000 deaths alone in the USA. We recommend combination therapy should be considered the gold standard of treatment for these patients and included in all future treatment guidelines.”

Statins have been used safely for years. They help to lower LDL-C "bad" cholesterol by reducing the production of it by the liver. Ezetimibe reduces the amount of cholesterol that the body takes from food by inhibiting the absorption of it in the intestines.

Article continues below

Some patients do not respond adequately to statins and are prescribed ezetimibe in combination with a statin. High doses of statins are known as "high intensity" statins and moderately high doses are known as "moderate intensity" or "medium intensity" statins.

Prof. Toth added: “Our findings underline the importance of the adages ‘the lower for better for longer’ but also the equally important ‘the earlier the better’ for treating patients at high risk of cardiovascular conditions and to avoid further medical complications and deaths.”

News.title: Headteacher seen on TV changed term dates for cruise holiday and used £30k school car
News.content:
Headteacher seen on TV changed term dates for cruise holiday and used £30k school car

A misconduct tribunal heard Joy Ballard, ex-principal of Ryde Academy on the Isle of Wight who starred on Channel 4 show Educating Cardiff, admitted allegations including changing term dates for cruise holidays and using a £30k school car for personal trips

View 2 Images Joy Ballard starred in Educating Cardiff in 2015 (Image: Mark Johnson. Channel 4 images must not be altered or manipulated in any way. This picture may be used solely for Channel 4 prog)

A headteacher has admitted to dishonesty after flouting school rules so she could go on a cruise holiday, among other rule breaks too.

‌



An award-winning former Isle of Wight headteacher altered her school's term dates to suit her private holidays, bought a karaoke machine using school funds, and commandeered a school vehicle for personal journeys, including a jaunt to France, a misconduct tribunal has been told.

‌



A virtual hearing examining the case of Joy Ballard, former principal of Ryde Academy, commenced on Friday and resumed today (Monday March 3).

‌



Mrs Ballard, who rose to fame in 2015 following her appearance on the Channel 4 programme, Educating Cardiff, opted not to attend, instead appointing Simon Pettet, from the NASUWT union, to represent her.

Concerns regarding Mrs Ballard's conduct were initially flagged on March 24, 2024, the tribunal was informed.

Inside the 'vile' Red vs Blue School Wars craze sparking knife crime panic in UK READ MORE :

View 2 Images Mrs Ballard has acknowledged all the factual allegations (Image: South Wales Echo)

‌



The panel, chaired by Richard Young, was presented with the following allegations against Mrs Ballard:.

Between July 2022 and June 2024, it was alleged that Mrs Ballard utilised a Peugeot 5008, acquired for £30,000 using school funds and intended for school business, for private journeys, including her daily commute, and/or for a personal family excursion to France around Easter 2024.

Around September 2023, Mrs Ballard modified the school term dates to fit around her personal holiday plans.

Mrs Ballard adjusted the dates at the conclusion of Autumn 2023 to accommodate a cruise she embarked upon. She also amended the start dates of Spring term 2024 for another cruise trip.

Mrs Ballard failed to attend school for the entirety of an inset day on January 3, 2024, as she was still away on a cruise holiday. She also missed a full school inset day on February 10, 2023.

From April 2021 through to October 2022, Mrs Ballard utilised school funds to buy camping gear, a karaoke machine and at least one television. None of these items proved particularly beneficial to the school.

During the period spanning September 2022 to June 2024, Mrs Ballard failed to adhere to proper protocols when banking cash. Instead, Mrs Ballard transported cash in her handbag, taking it home before depositing it.

Representing the Teaching Regulation Agency (TRA), Jim Olphert confirmed Mrs Ballard has acknowledged all the factual allegations and specific particulars.

Article continues below

She has additionally signed the statement of agreed facts, accepting that certain aspects of her behaviour constituted dishonesty and/or a lack of professional integrity, he stated.

Mrs Ballard assumed the role of principal at Ryde Academy, subsequently rebranded as Lift Ryde, on September 1, 2015. Her employment at the institution ended on August 31, 2024. The tribunal remains ongoing.

Do you have a story to share? Email niamh.kirk@reachplc.com

News.title: Lawns will be moss-free and 'thicker than ever' after Monty Don's 1 simple task in March
News.content:
Lawns will be moss-free and 'thicker than ever' after Monty Don's 1 simple task in March

Moss is a common sight on UK lawns in spring, but Monty Don has shared a simple method to get rid of the pesky problem and encourage healthy growth

View 2 Images Monty Don has a way to improve your lawn (Image: Getty)

March is here, and countless homeowners will be eager to address their lawns, which have likely become overgrown and moss-covered during the winter months.

‌



However, Monty Don, the celebrated gardening expert best known for hosting Gardeners' World, has outlined why it's better to focus on a single crucial task - nurturing grass vitality instead of attempting to eradicate moss.

‌



On his website, Monty explained: "The best grass likes very well-drained soil. Moss, for example, is always a symptom of poor drainage, made worse by shade. Unfortunately, even the best-prepared soil becomes compacted by matted roots, rain and, especially, normal family use. The answer is to work on it at least once a year by sticking a fork in the ground and wiggling it about and repeating the process every six inches or so."

‌



View 2 Images Homeowners will start to be thinking about prepping their lawns as Spring arrives (Image: Getty)

Moss takes hold on lawns plagued by waterlogging, thriving in moist environments, which signals that grass cannot drain properly due to compacted earth.

Compressed soil can lead to grass decay and promote disease, whilst simultaneously preventing nutrients and sunlight from penetrating to the grass roots, increasing the likelihood of uneven growth when spring arrives.

‌



Puncturing the ground with a garden fork aids lawn aeration, forming gaps in the soil that enable better air and water flow to reach the grass. Once you've thoroughly aerated the turf, boost growth by creating a simple homemade mixture that will break down naturally and feed the lawn with essential nutrients, reports the Express.

Monty recommended: "Mix up equal portions of sieved topsoil, sharp sand and sieved leaf mould or compost. If you do not have these things to hand, then just sharp or silver sand will do the job.

"Spread it across the area you have pricked and brush it in with a stiff broom, filling the holes with the mixture. This will help drainage and feed the grass."

‌



Applying mulch doesn't merely feed the soil but also naturally stops weeds and moss from taking hold on your turf, as it fortifies the grass, allowing it to dominate any competition.

Finally, make sure you also use a wire rake over your lawn, as it will help remove moss and any other organic material building up on your turf that's blocking nutrients from reaching the soil.

Monty said: "It is also worth giving the lawn a good scratch with a wire rake. This will get at all the overwintering thatch and moss, and let light and water get to the soil and to the roots of the grass.

Article continues below

"Put the debris on the compost and then mow. It will look a little bald for a week or so, but will grow back thicker than ever."

Taking the time to carry out these simple jobs in March will not only banish moss from your lawn but also promote the grass to develop more vigorously, guaranteeing you'll have a thick, green lawn to enjoy this spring.

News.title: Home Bargains confirms it will shut all 560 locations for 24 hours next month – check exact timings
News.content:
Home Bargains confirms it will shut all 560 locations for 24 hours next month – check exact timings

Home Bargains has announced it is closing 560 of the 630 UK shops for 24 hours - as people are told to check their local stores opening times ahead of visiting

View 2 Images Home Bargains has announced it is closing 550 shops - find out if you're affected (Image: Getty )

Bargain shoppers will need to plan in advance if they are hoping to visit a store soon.

‌



Popular discounter Home Bargains has announced the shutters will be pulled on 560 of their shops on Easter Sunday, April 5. However 70 stores in Scotland will remain open as usual.

‌



The chain employs around 28,000 staff all around the UK, and the closure is intended to give the workers a rare day off during the Easter period.

‌



The shops will still be open on Good Friday, however the hours reduced to 9am-7pm, and will also be open on Easter Monday trading from 9am-7pm.

View 2 Images Shoppers are urged to check the opening times before visiting Home Bargains shops (Image: Liverpool Echo)

A spokesperson told The Sun: "All Home Bargains stores in England and Wales will be closed on Easter Sunday." This comes under the rules set out in the Sunday Trading Act 1994, which requires larger shops over 280 square metres to shut on Easter Sunday. The same legislation also forces big stores to close on Christmas Day to ensure staff are given appropriate time off.

‌



However smaller stores such as convenience stores will be open as they are exempt from this law and can decide if they want to welcome customers. Meanwhile airport outlets, railway station shops, service stations and registered pharmacies are also permited to remain open.

Farms selling their own produce, motor supply outlets and suppliers of aircraft goods are among the other businesses that can continue operating too. Because of the Home Bargains closure, shoppers should check their stores opening hours before visiting, by checking social media or calling ahead to make sure.

It comes after a fresh wave of store closures has hit UK high streets in February 2026. W ith major chains including House of Fraser and Poundland amongst those closing their doors. With escalating expenses, diminished consumer spending, and difficult trading environments, businesses remain under pressure to stay viable.

‌



Poundland has already closed numerous branches nationwide as part of its restructuring strategy, with 32 additional outlets scheduled to shut in early 2026. According to The Metro, Poundland stores that have or are due to close this month are:

Chichester

Feltham

Crayford

Nottingham (Chilwell)

Urmston

Full list of Poundland stores set to close in 2026:

Lancaster, Lancashire

Northampton Sixfields, Northamptonshire

Weston-Super-Mare, Somerset

Hammersmith, Greater London

Prestatyn, Denbighshire

Faversham, Kent

Liverpool, Merseyside

Yeovil, Somerset

Nottingham Eastpoint, Nottinghamshire

Lymington, Hampshire

Christchurch (47 High Street), Dorset

Bristol Avon Meads, Bristol

Winton, Dorset

Coatbridge, North Lanarkshire, Scotland

Christchurch (Meteor Retail Park), Dorset

Arnold, Nottinghamshire

Worthing, West Sussex

Droitwich, Worcestershire

Ballymena, County Antrim, Northern Ireland

Oldham, Greater Manchester

Weston Favell, Northamptonshire

Portishead, Somerset

Grantham, Lincolnshire

Portadown, County Armagh, Northern Ireland

Farnham, Surrey

Brighton, East Sussex

Hempstead Valley, Kent

Bexhill, East Sussex

Ponders End, Greater London

Kilmarnock, East Ayrshire, Scotland

Mitcham, Greater London

Cameron Toll, Edinburgh, Scotland

Article continues below

While Claire's Accessories and The Original Factory Shop went into administration in January 2026 after Modella Capital stated attempts to save the brands had collapsed. The development threatens 1,355 positions across 154 Claire's Accessories branches in the UK and Ireland.

With over 280 UK sites, Claire's recorded losses of approximately £25 million in recent years. A spokesperson for the brand's owner said: "Very sadly, we have had to initiate insolvency proceedings for The Original Factory Shop (TOFS) and Claire's Accessories UK & Ireland. This has been a very tough decision," reported the Metro.

"We have worked intensively in an effort to save both businesses, having made last-ditch attempts to rescue them, but neither has a realistic possibility of trading profitably again. In these circumstances, administration is the only option. In both cases, the legacy effects of trading prior to our ownership left them highly vulnerable.

News.title: Fears for former WHSmith's high street shops as new owner seeks help
News.content:
Fears for former WHSmith's high street shops as new owner seeks help

Private equity firm Modella Capital has brought in advisers at Teneo to come up with a restructuring plan for the business - now trading as TG Jones - with roughly 80 stores believed to be most at risk

View 2 Images TG Jones, formerly known as WHSmith, has brought in troubleshooters for the business

The proprietors of WHSmith's former high street operation have brought in a squad of corporate troubleshooters less than 12 months after acquiring the chain.

‌



Private equity firm Modella Capital has enlisted advisers from Teneo to devise a restructuring blueprint that will place the business – now operating under the TG Jones banner – on firmer ground.

‌



The development will spark concerns about another wave of shop closures and redundancies at a time when Britain's high streets are already buckling under a succession of retail failures, including multiple chains within Modella's portfolio.

‌



It's understood that approximately 80 TG Jones outlets from a total estate of roughly 480 face the greatest risk following a challenging trading period. The fate of dozens more could hang in the balance unless more favourable rental agreements can be hammered out with landlords, according to The Telegraph.

View 2 Images WH Smith, a stalwart of the British High Street disappeared after the company agreed a sale

Final decisions remain outstanding. Whilst a shop closure scheme represents one possibility under consideration, Teneo is anticipated to assess other options including seeking financial backing from WH Smith.

‌



Modella's flexibility is constrained by an arrangement between both parties that essentially prevents it from shutting down underperforming locations for a year following its £40m acquisition. WH Smith divested the business last June, drawing the curtain on a 230-year fixture on Britain's high streets.

Retail industry insiders suggested that chronic underinvestment during its time under the previous parent company, Rachel Reeves's tax increases and a deteriorating economic climate had collectively c it the TG Jones business.

Rising employer National Insurance contributions and the National Living Wage alone are believed to have piled several million pounds onto running costs. One industry insider suggested retailers were grappling with a "dark market" where even long-established heavyweights such as Primark and B&M Bargains were now facing difficulties.

‌



At the same time, supermarkets are capturing a larger portion of shoppers' spending, driven predominantly by stubbornly high food price inflation. Behind closed doors, Modella bosses acknowledge that the company also miscalculated the consequences of losing the right to keep the WH Smith branding above its stores following the acquisition.

Trading at outlets still awaiting conversion to TG Jones has performed more strongly than those already carrying the new name. The private equity firm has sought to revive the business's prospects by investing in store makeovers. One adviser described the shop portfolio as "one of, if not the most under-invested in the UK".

Altogether, 14 locations have benefited from fresh investment. Modella has become one of the most active buyers of struggling high street chains. Its track record, though, remains patchy.

Article continues below

Last month, the firm placed both The Original Factory Shop and Claire's Accessories into administration, decisions that resulted in 2,200 redundancies and prompted the closure of almost 300 shops. Modella declined to comment, reports the Telegraph.

News.title: Reason why we eat chocolate eggs at Easter - and the deeper meaning behind it
News.content:
Reason why we eat chocolate eggs at Easter - and the deeper meaning behind it

From medieval Lent traditions to the first chocolate eggs by Fry's and Cadbury in the 1870s, here's the history behind Easter's most beloved symbols and what the Easter Bunny really means

View 2 Images This is the reason why we eat Easter eggs and the meaning behind them (Image: Oliver Helbig via Getty Images)

The custom of marking Jesus Christ's resurrection with chocolate eggs might appear odd - yet it actually stems from a mediaeval practice. Today, Brits snap up approximately 80 million Easter eggs annually, marking the celebration with a sweet treat, but there is a deeper meaning.

‌



According to scripture, God's son was crucified on what we now know as Good Friday, before rising again on Easter Sunday. This yearly occasion, which lands on April 20 this year, is commemorated by Christians across the globe and is believed to have commenced in the second century.

‌



It's important to recognise that tales of resurrection linked to springtime existed before Christianity - including the story of goddess Inanna who journeyed into the underworld seeking her late husband, met her death, but was subsequently brought back to life.

‌



The myth suggests she was allowed to emerge into the earthly realm as the sun for half the year, before being compelled to descend back into the underworld throughout winter for the remaining six months.

Further stories of resurrection and spring imagery feature the Egyptian god Horus and the Greek God Dionysus. But how did all this evolve into our beloved chocolate eggs, and what's the Easter Bunny's role in proceedings?

View 2 Images Many people celebrate Easter with an Easter egg (Image: Getty)

‌



Why do we eat chocolate eggs at Easter?

Plenty of Britons who aren't religious still mark Easter, seizing the bank holidays and school half-terms as a chance to reunite with loved ones. There's also an argument that Easter has grown increasingly commercialised, with sweet manufacturers raking in millions through flogging confectionery.

Yet this tradition isn't purely a modern marketing ploy - it actually dates back to medieval times when consuming eggs throughout Lent was forbidden. According to English Heritage, eggs (the feathered variety, not chocolate) were presented to the church on Good Friday, whilst villagers commonly gave this then-prized delicacy to their lord.

Numerous donated eggs were embellished with elaborate patterns and coloured in striking hues. They became entwined with various superstitions too, including double yolks representing impending prosperity.

‌



Others held the belief that eating eggs over the Easter season would 'improve fertility and prevent sudden death'.

This custom slowly transformed into the arrival of chocolate eggs, pioneered by Fry's in 1873. Cadbury swiftly jumped on board, releasing their hollow chocolate egg merely two years afterwards.

Where did the Easter Bunny come from?

Much like Father Christmas and the festive season, the Easter Bunny shares no direct link with Christianity's Easter story. Nevertheless, there's substantial mystery surrounding the origins of this legendary creature, which is technically a hare - not a rabbit.

‌



Numerous historians believe the Easter Hare bounded across from Germany, with a 1572 document declaring: "Don't worry if the Easter Bunny escapes you. Should we miss his eggs, we will cook the nest."

Steeped in tradition and folklore, this mythical creature is believed to have hopped into Pennsylvania Dutch settlements in the US around 1757 and has been adorning magazine covers at Easter since 1899.

According to Scientific American, the hare has been associated with Mary for centuries. "Their association with virgin birth comes from the fact that hares – often conflated mistakenly with rabbits – are able to produce a second litter of offspring whilst still pregnant with the first," the magazine explains.

Article continues below

Since that time, the Easter Bunny has become firmly embedded in Easter celebrations, appearing as cupcakes and various sweet treats, including the iconic Lindt Bunny. The claim that the legend stems from Pagan origins has been debunked time and again, yet continues to resurface on social media each Easter.
